channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: What the Trump-Xi Summit Won't Solve
item.description: A conversation with Orville Schell.

item.title: CIA Director Ratcliffe’s Trip to Havana
item.description: The United States is offering to turn Cuba’s lights back on—for a price.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of May 9: U.S. President Donald Trump visits China, India and Kenya host summits, and several prime ministers have a rough time.

item.title: A ‘Lord of the Flies’ for Our Time
item.description: This is the perfect project for the creator of ‘Adolescence.’

item.title: The 156,000 and the 1.4 Billion
item.description: What Curaçao figured out about World Cup soccer that India still hasn’t.

item.title: War Is an International Crime. Why Does It Go Unpunished?
item.description: A sweeping legal history reveals how the international community failed to live up to the promises of Nuremberg.

item.title: I Was the Russian Commander in a War Game. This Is How I Defeated NATO.
item.description: Decision paralysis and divisions among alliance members were easy to exploit.

item.title: How well do anabolic steroids work?
item.description: Very. But beware the side-effects

item.title: The World Economy After the Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: If Donald Trump and Xi Jinping's Beijing summit produces a sustained Sino-American trade truce and a path to reopening the Strait of Hormuz, that will give the world economy something it has lacked for the past year and half: a reduction in tail risks. In a year when so much has gone wrong, that is a welcome prospect.

item.title: AUKUS and North Korea in the Indo-Pacific: 5 Years On
item.description: Pyongyang portrays AUKUS as a dangerous threat, but has it really influenced North Korea’s evolving grand strategy?

item.title: The Trump-Xi Summit Was Remarkably Banal
item.description: A more confident China is happy to downplay presidential visits.

item.title: The Fall of Victory Day in Kazakhstan
item.description: In Kazakhstan, although Victory Day celebrations still took place on May 9, the Soviet focus is increasingly being replaced by expressions of national pride.

item.title: The Bureaucratic Tax on Africa Policy
item.description: How Washington bargains away its Africa strategy to other regions.

item.title: What did Trump and Xi actually achieve?
item.description: Divergent accounts suggest little progress on trade, Taiwan, Iran and AI

item.title: California Capitalism Meets the Moment
item.description: The United States needs AI governance that protects workers without stifling innovation; tax strategies that actually address inequality; and innovation policies that do not kill the goose that lays the golden egg. If there is one place where these demands will be met, it is the Golden State.

item.title: Hungary’s Power Shift Opens Door for EU Sanctions on Israel
item.description: Freed from Viktor Orban’s veto, the bloc should expand its actions against Israeli encroachment in the West Bank.

item.title: India and the Netherlands: From Quiet Cooperation to Strategic Partnership?
item.description: Can India and the Netherlands transform their growing cooperation into a fully-fledged strategic partnership with a clear long-term vision?

item.title: Southeast Asia’s Counter-drone Efforts
item.description: Southeast Asian countries are taking steps to counter drone threats. What’s really needed is a multilayered defense network.

item.title: Pakistan and the Trump-Xi Summit in Beijing
item.description: Like other Global South countries, Pakistan would like to pursue cooperation with both the US and China. Will the Beijing summit facilitate that?

item.title: What Takaichi’s Hanoi Visit Reveals About Vietnam’s Critical Minerals Strategy
item.description: Vietnam’s critical minerals strategy applies the doctrine of strategic autonomy to a new sector. The visit by Japan’s PM shows what that approach can deliver and where it falls short.

item.title: How China’s Population Stopped Noticing Their Country Had Been Sealed
item.description: Three lockdowns conditioned a population to accept the airtight, but invisible, seal that followed

item.title: Poll: How the US Lost the ‘Hearts and Minds’ of People Worldwide
item.description: Three years ago, only a fraction of the global public held more favorable opinions of China than of the U.S. Today, China and even Russia have a better global image.

item.title: After Trump-Xi Summit, Taiwan Breathes a Sigh of Relief
item.description: There was no "grand bargain" on Taiwan, but Taipei is still waiting for Trump to approve the latest arms package.

item.title: Trump’s Lebanon Negotiations Are Breaking the Country
item.description: Rather than acting as a good-faith mediator, Trump is humiliating the Lebanese government.

item.title: AI’s Shadow Global Governance
item.description: Judging by most of the media coverage of the global AI "race," the United States and China are the two dominant players, and every other country and organization is at risk of being left behind. The reality is far more complex—and not nearly as bleak.

item.title: What Kim Jong Un Really Fears: Outside Information
item.description: Since COVID-19, the grounds for execution in North Korea have been shifting away from ordinary violent crime and toward outside information, religion, and political dissent.

item.title: Argentina Has Milei Malaise
item.description: As wages in the country slump, so do the libertarian leader’s ratings.

item.title: The Global Water Cycle Is Critical Infrastructure
item.description: Governments and financial institutions urgently need to align on restoring and maintaining the planet's life-support system. By harnessing recent advances in science, monitoring technologies, and data availability, we can finally start treating water like the shared, transnational asset that it is.

item.title: Was Xi Jinping Inevitable?
item.description: Under Xi Jinping, China is moving steadily away from the pragmatic authoritarianism of the reform era toward the restoration of Mao-style totalitarianism. Two recent books argue that Xi's political ascent was no historical accident, but a structural feature of China’s political system.

item.title: Myanmar’s Junta Cries Foul as Kim Aris Pleads For Proof His Mother Is Alive
item.description: Rights groups say ASEAN and the international community must have direct access to Aung San Suu Kyi.

item.title: Why Did West Bengal Decline, and Can the BJP Revive It?
item.description: The state’s woes are of its own making. Since independence, successive governments implemented policies that repelled foreign investment.

item.title: What Makes Trump’s New Counterterrorism Strategy So Alarming
item.description: Most worrisome are the terrorist threats missing from the document.

item.title: Can Corporate America Protect Democracy?
item.description: In the Trump era, CEOs need to define redlines.

item.title: How Europe Found Its Nerve
item.description: Trump's overreach has finally forged continental unity.

item.title: While Trump Sought Business Deals, Beijing Came Prepared to Redefine China-US Relations
item.description: Why the new frame of “constructive China-U.S. relationship of strategic stability” matters.

item.title: Can the NPT Survive Amid Global Disorder?
item.description: The 2026 NPT Review Conference is unfolding against a backdrop of geopolitical upheaval. Absent strong U.S. leadership inside and outside the conference, even modest success appears remote.
